items.title: Phase2-linux-shell: Linux Tauri shell with WebKitGTK
items.intent: Markdown runs on Linux as a first-class citizen — native file dialogs via GTK, XDG desktop integration, .desktop file, and AppStream metadata. A macOS-only editor violates D-PLAT-1.
items.description:
Create the Linux Tauri shell. The Tauri project already builds for macOS; this item extends it to produce a Linux binary using WebKitGTK. Add a .desktop file for application launcher integration, AppStream metadata for software center discoverability, and verify GTK file dialogs work correctly.

Scope boundary: this item produces a working Linux build with desktop integration. Flatpak packaging is a separate concern within this phase but can be a follow-up item if needed.
steps: Configure Tauri to build for Linux target with WebKitGTK backend
Add a .desktop file (XDG Desktop Entry) to the project with correct categories, icon, and MIME type associations for .md files
Add AppStream metadata XML for software center integration
Verify the Linux build produces a runnable binary that opens a window with the CodeMirror editor
Verify GTK file dialogs (open/save) work correctly on Linux
Test that all existing features (open, edit, save, themes, find/replace) work identically on Linux
Add Linux build target to CI

acceptanceCriteria.text: cargo tauri build --target linux produces a binary
.desktop file exists with correct MIME type for .md files

acceptanceCriteria: GTK file dialogs open and return selected file paths correctly
Editor renders and accepts input identically to the macOS build
items.groomingNotes: Third re-groom. Root cause of 3 failures: prior groomingNotes claimed verify commands were fixed but JSON was never updated. Fixed now: (1) build AC checks for build-linux job in CI workflow — sprint must add this job, not cross-compile on macOS; (2) test AC tail pipe removed — was swallowing exit code. Desktop and AppStream ACs check file presence which is correct since the sprint creates these files.

items.title: Phase2-windows-shell: Windows Tauri shell with WebView2
items.intent: Markdown runs on Windows as a first-class citizen — native file dialogs, MSI installer, and WinGet manifest. Windows is the largest desktop platform and must be supported before the first public pre-release.
items.description:
Create the Windows Tauri shell. Tauri uses WebView2 (Edge Chromium) on Windows. This item produces a Windows build with an MSI installer and a WinGet manifest for package manager distribution.

Scope boundary: this item produces a working Windows build with installer. Microsoft Store submission is a future item.
steps: Configure Tauri to build for Windows target with WebView2 backend
Configure the MSI installer bundle in tauri.conf.json (app name, icon, file associations for .md)
Create a WinGet manifest for community submission
Verify the Windows build produces a runnable .exe and .msi
Verify Windows native file dialogs work correctly
Test that all existing features work identically on Windows
Add Windows build target to CI

acceptanceCriteria.text: cargo tauri build produces an MSI installer on Windows
File association for .md is configured in tauri.conf.json

acceptanceCriteria: Windows native file dialogs open and return selected file paths correctly
Editor renders and accepts input identically to macOS and Linux builds
WinGet manifest exists and is syntactically valid
items.groomingNotes: Re-groom. Prior groom claimed fixes but verify commands were not applied to JSON. Fixed: (1) build AC now checks CI has windows-latest job (sprint must add it, none exists yet); (2) test AC tail pipe removed; (3) WinGet manifest verify uses find. File association verify checks for .md in tauri.conf.json.

items.title: Phase2-flatpak: Flatpak packaging for Linux distribution
items.intent: Markdown is installable via Flatpak on any Linux distribution. Flatpak is the most widely supported universal Linux packaging format and is required for software center visibility on GNOME, KDE, and elementary OS.
items.description:
Create a Flatpak manifest for the Markdown editor. The manifest should build from the Tauri project, bundle all dependencies (WebKitGTK runtime from the GNOME SDK), and produce an installable Flatpak.

Scope boundary: this item produces a buildable Flatpak manifest and verifies it installs and runs. Flathub submission is a separate follow-up.
steps: Create a Flatpak manifest YAML/JSON referencing the GNOME SDK and WebKitGTK runtime
Configure the manifest to build the Tauri project from source
Add .desktop file and AppStream metadata to the Flatpak build
Build the Flatpak locally and verify it installs
Verify the Flatpak-sandboxed app can open files via the XDG portal file dialog
Add Flatpak build to CI as an optional job
acceptanceCriteria: flatpak-builder produces an installable Flatpak bundle
The Flatpak-installed app opens a window and accepts editor input
File open dialog works through the XDG portal (Flatpak sandbox)
items.groomingNotes: Blocked on FEAT-022 (Linux shell). Verify commands look correct for file-existence checks. flatpak-builder build and runtime install ACs are manual (require the Flatpak toolchain). Mark ready only after FEAT-022 passes.

items.title: Phase3-voice-intent: Voice-intent command input
items.intent: The user can speak a command ('make this paragraph shorter', 'add a heading here', 'summarize this section') and the editor executes it as an AI action. Voice is a natural input modality for prose editing — faster than typing a prompt, more expressive than a menu item.
items.description:
Use platform speech APIs (Web Speech API in the webview, or native speech recognition via Tauri plugins) to capture voice input. Convert speech to text, interpret it as an intent (using the local or BYO-key AI model), and execute the corresponding editor action.

Activation: hold a key (e.g. Fn or a configurable shortcut) to activate voice input. Release to submit. Show a visual indicator while listening.

Scope boundary: this item implements voice-to-intent. The AI actions it triggers are defined in FEAT-029/FEAT-030. This item does not implement dictation (continuous speech-to-text for document content).
steps: Evaluate speech recognition options: Web Speech API (Chrome only), Tauri native plugin, or platform APIs
Implement voice capture with push-to-talk activation (hold key to listen, release to submit)
Show a visual indicator (microphone icon, waveform) while listening
Convert captured audio to text using the chosen speech API
Pass the transcribed text to the AI model as an intent to interpret
Map the AI's interpretation to an editor action and execute it
Handle errors: microphone permission denied, speech not recognized, AI interpretation failed
acceptanceCriteria: Holding the activation key shows a listening indicator
Speaking a command ('make this shorter') and releasing the key executes an AI action on the selected text
Microphone permission is requested on first use with a clear explanation
If speech recognition is unavailable, the feature is absent and the editor works normally

items.title: Phase4-ios: iOS Tauri mobile shell
items.intent: Markdown runs on iOS as a first-class mobile citizen — Files app integration, share sheet, swipe gestures, and UIKit sheets. The primary persona mixes desktop and mobile; cross-platform includes mobile.
items.description:
Create the iOS Tauri mobile shell using Tauri 2.0's iOS support. The shell wraps the same CodeMirror 6 editor in a WKWebView with iOS-specific integration: Files app file provider for opening .md files, share sheet for receiving .md files from other apps, and platform-idiomatic gesture support.

Scope boundary: this item produces a working iOS app that can open, edit, and save markdown files. AI features on iOS are gated on Phase 3 completing first. App Store submission is a separate follow-up.
steps: Configure Tauri for iOS target (Tauri 2.0 mobile support)
Set up Xcode project configuration (bundle ID, signing, capabilities)
Implement Files app integration via UIDocumentPickerViewController or Tauri's file dialog plugin for iOS
Add share sheet / intent filter support for receiving .md files
Verify CodeMirror 6 renders correctly in iOS WKWebView (viewport, keyboard avoidance, safe areas)
Test touch interactions: selection, scrolling, pinch-to-zoom text
Verify the on-screen keyboard does not obscure the editor content (keyboard avoidance)

acceptanceCriteria.text: cargo tauri ios build produces an iOS app bundle

acceptanceCriteria: The iOS app opens and displays the CodeMirror 6 editor
File open via Files app integration works
File save writes back to the original file location
The on-screen keyboard does not obscure the editing area
Text selection and scrolling work via touch
items.groomingNotes: Re-groom. Prior groomingNotes claimed tail pipe was removed from build verify but change was not applied to JSON. Fixed: verify is now cargo tauri ios build with no pipe, exit code propagates correctly. Remaining ACs are intentionally manual — require device testing with Xcode + iOS SDK.

items.title: Phase4-android: Android Tauri mobile shell
items.intent: Markdown runs on Android — Storage Access Framework integration, intent filters for .md files, Material design patterns. Android is the largest mobile platform globally.
items.description:
Create the Android Tauri mobile shell using Tauri 2.0's Android support. The shell wraps the same CodeMirror 6 editor in an Android WebView with platform integration: Storage Access Framework for file access, intent filters for opening .md files from file managers, and Material design patterns.

Scope boundary: this item produces a working Android app. Play Store submission is a separate follow-up.
steps: Configure Tauri for Android target (Tauri 2.0 mobile support)
Set up Android project configuration (package name, signing, permissions)
Implement file access via Storage Access Framework (ACTION_OPEN_DOCUMENT, ACTION_CREATE_DOCUMENT)
Add intent filter for .md MIME type so the app appears in 'Open with' for markdown files
Verify CodeMirror 6 renders correctly in Android WebView (viewport, keyboard avoidance)
Test touch interactions: selection, scrolling, long-press context menu
Verify the on-screen keyboard does not obscure the editor content

acceptanceCriteria.text: cargo tauri android build produces an APK

acceptanceCriteria: The Android app opens and displays the CodeMirror 6 editor
File open via Storage Access Framework works
File save writes back to the original file location
The on-screen keyboard does not obscure the editing area
The app appears in 'Open with' menu for .md files
items.groomingNotes: Re-groom. Prior groomingNotes claimed tail pipe was removed from build verify but change was not applied to JSON. Fixed: verify is now cargo tauri android build with no pipe, exit code propagates correctly. Remaining ACs are intentionally manual — require device/emulator testing with Android SDK.

items.title: Phase5-extended-doctor: Extended doctor diagnostic rules
items.intent: The document doctor catches more structural and stylistic issues: passive voice, orphaned sections, inconsistent list markers. The doctor grows from a basic tool into a genuinely useful writing assistant.
items.description:
Extend the doctor engine (FEAT-011) with additional diagnostic rules:

- **Passive voice detection**: flag sentences using passive constructions, with a suggestion to rephrase
- **Orphaned sections**: detect heading-only sections with no content between them
- **Inconsistent list markers**: detect mixing of -, *, and + within the same list level
- **Long paragraphs**: flag paragraphs over a configurable word count (default 300 words) as potentially needing a break

All rules are on by default but individually disablable. Rules produce Diagnostic structs with span, severity, rule ID, and message, consistent with FEAT-011's architecture.
steps: Implement passive voice detection rule in markdown-core doctor module
Implement orphaned sections rule (heading followed immediately by another heading with no content)
Implement inconsistent list markers rule
Implement long paragraph rule with configurable threshold
Add unit tests for each new rule
Wire new rules into the doctor's rule registry
Surface diagnostics in the editor via CodeMirror lint or inline decorations
acceptanceCriteria: Passive voice constructions are flagged with a suggestion
Empty sections (heading with no content before next heading) are flagged
Mixed list markers within the same list level are flagged
Long paragraphs are flagged with the word count

items.title: Phase5-mermaid: Mermaid diagram rendering and AI-assisted editing
items.intent: Mermaid fenced code blocks render as interactive diagrams inline in the editor. AI can assist with creating and editing Mermaid syntax. AI-generated documents with diagrams render beautifully on open — this is a key claim in the press release.
items.description:
Render Mermaid diagrams inline in the editor when a fenced code block is tagged with ```mermaid. Use the Mermaid.js library to render the diagram as an SVG, displayed via a CodeMirror widget decoration that replaces the code block in rich view (or shows below it in source view).

AI-assisted Mermaid editing: when the user selects a Mermaid block and invokes an AI action, the AI can modify the Mermaid syntax and the diagram re-renders live.

Scope boundary: rendering and basic AI editing. No visual drag-and-drop diagram editor.
steps: Add mermaid.js as an npm dependency
Detect ```mermaid fenced code blocks in the CodeMirror syntax tree
Render the Mermaid syntax to SVG using mermaid.render()
Display the rendered SVG as a CodeMirror widget decoration below or replacing the code block
Re-render on content change within the mermaid block (debounced)
In rich view (The Render), replace the code block entirely with the rendered diagram
Wire AI actions to understand and modify Mermaid syntax
acceptanceCriteria: A ```mermaid code block renders as an SVG diagram in the editor
Editing the Mermaid syntax updates the diagram in real time
Invalid Mermaid syntax shows an error message instead of a broken diagram

items.title: Phase5-math: Math rendering with KaTeX
items.intent: Inline ($...$) and display ($$...$$) math expressions render beautifully in the editor. Math is a first-class content type in technical and academic markdown documents.
items.description:
Render LaTeX math expressions inline in the editor using KaTeX. Inline math ($...$) renders as styled inline elements. Display math ($$...$$) renders as centered block elements.

Use CodeMirror decorations: in source view, math delimiters are visible and the rendered output appears alongside or on hover. In rich view (The Render), delimiters are hidden and only the rendered math is shown.

Scope boundary: rendering only. No equation editor, no MathML export.
steps: Add katex as an npm dependency
Detect $...$ (inline) and $$...$$ (display) syntax in the editor
Render math expressions using katex.renderToString()
Display inline math as a CodeMirror widget decoration replacing the $...$ span
Display display math as a centered block widget decoration
Handle KaTeX errors gracefully: show the raw syntax with an error indicator
In rich view, hide delimiters and show only rendered math
acceptanceCriteria: Inline math ($x^2$) renders as a formatted math expression
Display math ($$\sum_{i=1}^n$$) renders as a centered block expression
Invalid LaTeX shows the raw syntax with an error indicator instead of crashing

items.title: Phase5-folding: Folding and outline view
items.intent: The user can fold sections of the document by heading level and navigate the document structure via an outline panel. Long documents become manageable without scrolling.
items.description:
Implement section folding and an outline/table-of-contents panel:

1. **Folding**: each heading creates a foldable region spanning to the next heading of equal or higher level. Click a gutter indicator or use a keyboard shortcut to fold/unfold. Folded regions show a placeholder (e.g. '... (N lines)').
2. **Outline panel**: a side panel showing the document's heading structure as a navigable tree. Clicking a heading in the outline scrolls to it in the editor. The outline updates live as the user edits.

Scope boundary: heading-level folding only. No arbitrary fold regions, no manual fold markers.
steps: Use CodeMirror's foldService to define fold ranges based on heading levels from the markdown syntax tree
Add fold gutters with click-to-fold indicators
Add keyboard shortcuts for fold/unfold (Cmd+Shift+[ / Cmd+Shift+])
Create an outline panel component showing the heading hierarchy
Wire outline clicks to scroll to the corresponding heading in the editor
Update the outline live on document changes
Add a toggle for showing/hiding the outline panel
acceptanceCriteria: Clicking a fold indicator in the gutter collapses the section under a heading
Folded sections show a placeholder with the number of hidden lines
The outline panel shows all headings in hierarchical order
Clicking a heading in the outline scrolls the editor to that heading

items.title: Phase5-multi-doc: Multi-document tabs and split view
items.intent: The user can open multiple documents in tabs within a single window and optionally split the view to see two documents side by side. This is the progression from the M0 one-file-per-window model to a productivity-oriented multi-document workflow.
items.description:
Add a tab bar to the editor window. Each tab holds an independent CodeMirror editor instance with its own document state. The user can:
- Open multiple files (each opens in a new tab)
- Switch between tabs with keyboard shortcuts (Cmd+Shift+] / Cmd+Shift+[)
- Close tabs (Cmd+W) with unsaved-changes prompt
- Drag tabs to reorder
- Split the view horizontally or vertically to show two tabs side by side

Scope boundary: tabs and split view. No file tree sidebar (that could be a separate item).
steps: Create a tab bar component above the editor area
Manage multiple editor instances, each with independent document state
Opening a file creates a new tab (or focuses existing tab if the file is already open)
Add tab switching shortcuts: Cmd+Shift+] (next), Cmd+Shift+[ (previous), Cmd+1-9 (by index)
Add close-tab behavior with unsaved-changes prompt
Implement drag-to-reorder for tabs
Add split view: a command to split the current view horizontally or vertically
Ensure file watching (FEAT-026) and auto-save (FEAT-025) work correctly per-tab
acceptanceCriteria: Opening multiple files creates separate tabs
Cmd+Shift+] and Cmd+Shift+[ switch between tabs
Closing a tab with unsaved changes shows a prompt
Split view shows two documents side by side

items.title: Phase5-quick-open: Quick Open fuzzy file search
items.intent: The user can press Cmd+P to fuzzy-search across all .md files in the current folder tree and open one instantly. This is the fast-switching workflow that power users expect from VS Code and Obsidian.
items.description:
Implement a Quick Open dialog triggered by Cmd+P (macOS) / Ctrl+P (other platforms). The dialog shows a text input that fuzzy-matches against all .md files in the directory tree containing the current file.

File discovery: walk the directory tree from the current file's parent directory (or a configured root), collect all .md file paths, and index them for fuzzy matching. Use a simple fuzzy matching algorithm (subsequence match with scoring for consecutive characters and path segment boundaries).

Scope boundary: file search and open. No content search (that would be a separate Cmd+Shift+F item).
steps: Implement a Tauri command that walks a directory tree and returns all .md file paths
Implement a fuzzy matching algorithm in TypeScript (subsequence match with scoring)
Create a Quick Open overlay UI: centered modal with text input and scrollable results list
Wire Cmd+P / Ctrl+P to open the Quick Open overlay
On result selection (Enter or click), open the file (in a new tab if FEAT-040 is implemented, otherwise in the current editor)
Cache the file list and refresh on focus (to catch new files)
Show the relative path of each result for disambiguation
acceptanceCriteria: Cmd+P opens a Quick Open overlay
Typing in the overlay fuzzy-matches against .md files in the directory tree
Selecting a result opens the file
Escape closes the overlay without navigation

items.title: Phase5-images: Image handling — inline rendering, drag-drop, paste
items.intent: Images in markdown (![alt](path)) render inline in the editor. Users can drag-drop or paste images and have them saved to a sensible location with the markdown reference inserted automatically. AI-generated documents with images render beautifully on open.
items.description:
Implement image handling in the editor:

1. **Inline rendering**: ![alt](path) references render the image inline as a CodeMirror widget decoration. Relative paths resolve from the current file's directory. URLs render via fetch.
2. **Drag-drop**: dragging an image file onto the editor saves it to the same directory as the current file (or a configurable images/ subdirectory) and inserts ![](relative-path) at the drop position.
3. **Paste**: pasting an image from the clipboard saves it (with a timestamp-based filename) and inserts the reference.
4. **Error handling**: broken image references show a placeholder with the alt text and path.

Scope boundary: local and URL images. No image editing, no resizing handles, no image optimization.
steps: Detect ![alt](path) syntax in the CodeMirror syntax tree
Resolve image paths relative to the current file's directory
Render images as CodeMirror widget decorations (replace the syntax with the rendered image in rich view)
Implement drag-drop handler: intercept drop events with image files, save to disk, insert markdown reference
Implement paste handler: intercept paste events with image data, save to disk, insert markdown reference
Handle broken images: show alt text and path as a placeholder
Add max-width styling so large images don't break the layout
acceptanceCriteria: ![alt](path) with a valid local image renders the image inline
Dragging an image file onto the editor inserts a markdown image reference
Pasting an image from the clipboard saves it and inserts a reference
Broken image references show a placeholder, not a broken image icon

items.title: Phase5-pdf: Render, print, and share as polished PDF
items.intent: The user can export the current document as a beautifully typeset PDF or print it directly. The PDF output should look polished — proper typography, rendered diagrams and math, styled code blocks — not a raw text dump.
items.description:
Implement PDF export and print for the current document:

1. **PDF export** (Cmd+Shift+E or File > Export as PDF): render the markdown to a styled HTML document, then convert to PDF using the webview's print-to-PDF capability or a library like Puppeteer/Playwright (headless).
2. **Print** (Cmd+P with Shift, or File > Print): open the OS print dialog with a print-formatted view of the document.

The output should include: styled headings, bold/italic, rendered code blocks with syntax highlighting, rendered Mermaid diagrams (if FEAT-037), rendered math (if FEAT-038), and images.

Scope boundary: single-document export. No batch export, no custom templates.
steps: Render the current markdown document to styled HTML (reuse The Render's decoration output or a separate render pipeline)
Add a print stylesheet that produces clean, typeset output
Implement Cmd+Shift+E: render to HTML and use the webview's print-to-PDF to save as PDF
Implement File > Print: open the OS print dialog with the styled HTML
Include rendered diagrams and math if those features are available
Add page break hints before top-level headings
acceptanceCriteria: Cmd+Shift+E exports a PDF file to the user's chosen location
The PDF includes styled headings, bold/italic, and code blocks
File > Print opens the OS print dialog with formatted content
The PDF does not contain raw markdown syntax — it is a rendered view

items.title: Phase5-custom-themes: Custom themes and fonts
items.intent: Users can customize the editor's appearance beyond the built-in light and dark themes — choose fonts, adjust colors, or install community-created themes. Personalization without a plugin system.
items.description:
Add a theme customization system:

1. **Font selection**: let the user choose body and code fonts from system-installed fonts, with a preview
2. **Color scheme customization**: let the user adjust key colors (background, text, accent, heading colors) within a theme
3. **Community themes**: support loading theme files (JSON or CSS) from a directory (~/.config/markdown/themes/)

Theme files define CSS custom property overrides. The built-in light and dark themes are the defaults; user themes override specific properties.

Scope boundary: visual customization only. No editor behavior changes via themes. No theme marketplace.
steps: Define a theme file format (JSON mapping CSS custom property names to values)
Load user themes from ~/.config/markdown/themes/ on startup
Add a Preferences > Appearance pane with font selection (body and code fonts)
Add color customization controls for key properties (background, text, accent, headings)
Apply theme overrides via CSS custom properties on the root element
Provide a theme preview in the preferences pane
Ensure custom themes work with both light and dark base themes
acceptanceCriteria: A JSON theme file in the themes directory is loaded and applied on startup
Font selection in Preferences changes the editor's body and code fonts
Color customization changes are reflected immediately in the editor
The built-in themes still work when no custom theme is present

items.title: Roll back AI — gate behind off-by-default cargo feature and remove from UI surface
items.intent: PRODUCT D-ROAD-3 and ARCHITECTURE A-AI-DEFERRED commit to shipping AI after v1.0, not before. FEAT-029/030/032 violated that. We want the editor to stop presenting AI as a feature so focus returns to the core reading/writing experience, without throwing away the work — the code stays behind an opt-out flag for when the AI phase formally begins.
items.description:
Gate the llama.cpp inference engine and all ai_* Tauri commands behind the existing 'ai' cargo feature, and DROP the feature from the default build. Remove aiExtension() registration from src/editor.ts. Remove the AI settings pane from the settings modal (keep BYO-key storage code dormant but unreachable from UI). Remove completionsExtension() registration. The keychain-stored BYO keys must remain untouched on disk for users who already set them. Document the rollback in docs/ARCHITECTURE.md §5 so the AI section matches reality: the constraints stay locked, the implementation is dormant.

Scope boundary: no code deletion. This is a visibility/surface rollback, not a git revert. The AI crate features, commands, and modules stay in the tree behind conditional compilation so re-enabling is a one-line build flag change when the AI phase begins.
steps: Remove 'ai' from the default features list in src-tauri/Cargo.toml and markdown-core/Cargo.toml
Wrap all ai_* Tauri command registrations in #[cfg(feature = "ai")] in src-tauri/src/lib.rs
Remove aiExtension() and completionsExtension() from the extensions array in src/editor.ts
Remove the AI section from the settings modal UI in src/settings.ts (leave keychain read/write functions intact but unreferenced)
Remove any AI-related status-bar indicators and keyboard shortcuts from the default build
Add a short note to docs/ARCHITECTURE.md §5 explaining the code is gated off pending D-ROAD-3
Verify cargo build --workspace (no --features ai) succeeds and produces a binary with no AI surface
Run the app, confirm no AI menu items, no ghost-text suggestions, no settings pane entries

acceptanceCriteria.text: Default workspace build has no ai feature enabled
aiExtension is not registered in the default editor
completionsExtension is not registered in the default editor

acceptanceCriteria: Running the default build shows no AI-related UI (no ghost text, no AI settings pane, no AI menu items)
items.groomingNotes: Solo-dev rollback. Not a revert — cfg-gated so AI phase can re-enable with a feature flag. Watch that the llama-cpp-2 crate does not get pulled in by the default build (check cargo tree after). BYO-key keychain data untouched.
items.lastFailReason: Baseline validation failed before developer ran

items.title: Restore committed baseline.json and referenced engineering artifacts
items.intent: ARCHITECTURE A-PROC-2 makes docs/baseline.json permanent — it is the regression gate's source of truth. Current git status shows it deleted along with docs/engine-comparison.json, docs/engine-decision.md, docs/commonmark_status.md, and docs/large-file-performance.md. The regression CI job cannot work without baseline.json, and ARCHITECTURE §3.1 cites the engine artifacts as the defense for A-CORE-1. Either restore them or remove the references.
items.description:
Recover docs/baseline.json (the canonical ubuntu-latest-measured numbers) and the engine-decision artifacts. Preferred path: run the baseline-capture workflow to regenerate baseline.json on the CI runner and commit. For the deleted engine docs, restore from git history if possible, otherwise rewrite brief summaries sufficient to defend A-CORE-1 and document large-file behavior.

Scope boundary: this item restores the artifacts. Extending the baseline to the four-slice distribution described in ARCHITECTURE §2 is FEAT-047, not this item.
steps: git log --diff-filter=D to find the commit that deleted each file
git checkout <commit>^ -- docs/baseline.json docs/engine-comparison.json docs/engine-decision.md docs/commonmark_status.md docs/large-file-performance.md
If baseline.json values are stale, trigger the Capture Baseline workflow on main to regenerate
Verify scripts/measure_baseline.sh --check runs cleanly against the restored file
Commit the restored files

acceptanceCriteria.text: docs/baseline.json exists and is valid JSON
scripts/measure_baseline.sh --check exits 0 against the restored baseline

items.groomingNotes: These files were deleted in working tree per git status — they have never been committed-as-deleted. git checkout HEAD -- docs/<file> may be enough. If the Capture Baseline workflow needs to rerun, do that on CI not locally.

items.title: Baseline corpus with small/medium/large/session slices and per-slice regression gate
items.intent: ARCHITECTURE §2 requires the regression gate to fire independently on four representative load slices so that a 12% regression on large-document open time blocks a merge even when small-document numbers look fine. Current measure_baseline.sh only captures open_small_ms and open_10k_ms — no 25 KB medium, no synthetic session, no committed corpus. Without the distribution, D-PERF-1 is not actually enforced.
items.description:
Create docs/baseline-corpus/ with four representative fixtures: small (~1 KB, ~30 lines), medium (~25 KB, ~800 lines including tables), large (~250 KB, ~8000 lines with rich content), and a synthetic session script (open → scroll → edit → format → save across mixed sizes). Extend scripts/measure_baseline.sh and the measure binary so baseline.json captures each core metric (open, keystroke latency, format, save, reopen) per slice, N≥5 medians. The regression gate must fire on any slice independently at 110% of its committed median.

Scope boundary: CJK/RTL/emoji fixtures are a later addition — this item establishes the distribution infrastructure on English content.
steps: Create docs/baseline-corpus/ with small.md, medium.md, large.md and commit
Write a session-script fixture (JSON or shell) describing the synthetic session sequence
Extend markdown-core/bin/measure.rs to accept a corpus path and emit per-slice metrics
Extend docs/baseline.json schema to store per-slice medians
Update scripts/measure_baseline.sh --capture and --check to iterate slices and compare per-slice
Update the baseline-capture CI workflow if the invocation changed
Verify the check fails loudly when an artificial regression is injected into one slice

acceptanceCriteria.verify: test -f docs/baseline-corpus/small.md && test -f docs/baseline-corpus/medium.md && test -f docs/baseline-corpus/large.md

acceptanceCriteria: Injecting a 15% regression in one slice's open time causes --check to exit non-zero while other slices stay within bounds
items.groomingNotes: Depends on FEAT-046 (baseline.json restored first). The synthetic session is the trickiest piece — aim for a scripted driver that exercises steady-state, not cold-start. File size targets: small ~1KB/30 lines, medium ~25KB/800 lines with tables, large ~250KB/8000 lines with rich content.

items.title: CI build matrix — add build-linux, build-windows, build-web jobs
items.intent: PRODUCT D-PLAT-1 says every platform is first-class. Current .github/workflows/build.yml has only build-macos and baseline-check. FEAT-022 (Linux) and FEAT-023 (Windows) have failed groom three times because their ACs depend on CI jobs that do not exist. Adding the build matrix unblocks those items and makes cross-platform regressions visible at PR time.
items.description:
Extend .github/workflows/build.yml with three additional jobs: build-linux (ubuntu-latest, install webkit2gtk-4.1 dev packages, cargo build + tauri build), build-windows (windows-latest, cargo build + tauri build, artifact the MSI), and build-web (ubuntu-latest, npm install + vite build --config vite.config.web.ts, artifact dist-web). Each job runs cargo test --workspace where applicable. The jobs run in parallel with build-macos; failures in any block PR merge.

Scope boundary: this item adds the jobs and proves they go green on a trivial PR. Platform-specific features (Spotlight integration, XDG registration, MSI file associations) live in FEAT-022/023.
steps: Add build-linux job: ubuntu-latest, apt-get install libwebkit2gtk-4.1-dev build-essential libssl-dev, then cargo build --workspace and cargo test --workspace
Add build-windows job: windows-latest, cargo build --workspace and cargo test --workspace
Add build-web job: ubuntu-latest, npm ci and npx vite build --config vite.config.web.ts, upload dist-web as artifact
Ensure all four build jobs run in parallel on pull_request and push to main
Verify the workflow file is valid YAML and commits trigger the new jobs
acceptanceCriteria: All four build jobs run on a test PR and the matrix completes (may have failures — that's a subsequent fix, not this item's scope)
items.groomingNotes: Unblocks FEAT-022/023. webkit2gtk-4.1 is the Tauri 2 Linux dependency (not -4.0). If the Linux or Windows build fails on first run due to Tauri-specific setup, file a sub-bug rather than letting this item fail groom — the goal here is the matrix existing, not every platform going green on day one.

items.title: Read-mode default view with click-to-enter author mode
items.intent: PRODUCT D-UX-1 and DP-3 make read mode the default view when a .md file opens. This is the single most load-bearing product claim in the press release — 'open any .md file and see it rendered beautifully … click to edit and you're in a polished author environment.' Today the editor lands users in author mode. FEAT-015 shipped a source-to-rich toggle animation, but that is an effect on the author surface, not a default read-first mode. Until this ships, the product does not match its own pitch.
items.description:
Build read mode as a distinct EditorView state over the same document. On file open, mount the read view: all rendered content visible (headings styled, tables laid out, code blocks highlighted, images inline where FEAT-042 ships that), source characters (#, **, backticks) hidden via widget decorations, selection and copy work on rendered text. A single click or tap anywhere in the document transitions to author mode with the cursor placed at the click position. A visible affordance (keyboard shortcut + optional toolbar button) returns to read mode. The transition uses CSS crossfade; prefers-reduced-motion gets an instant crossfade.

Scope boundary: this item ships read mode for the content types already rendered in author mode (headings, emphasis, code spans, code blocks, lists, tables, links). Mermaid, math, and images are later items (FEAT-037/038/042) and integrate via whatever rendering hook this item exposes. Remembering per-file last-used mode is out of scope here — can be a follow-up.
steps: Design the read-mode view as a CodeMirror EditorView variant — reuse WYSIWYM decoration infrastructure in src/wysiwym.ts but apply it globally (cursor-proximity rule does not apply in read mode)
Add a readModeExtension() in a new src/read-mode.ts that hides all syntax marks, disables caret/editing, and enables a single-click handler that dispatches a mode change
Wire the mode state into the top-level app: on file open, mount read mode by default; add Cmd+E (or equivalent) keyboard shortcut to toggle modes
Implement the mode transition: CSS crossfade, Reduced Motion fallback to instant swap
Place cursor at click position when transitioning read→author
Verify keyboard-only users can enter and exit read mode via shortcut (a11y)
Verify screen-reader semantics: read mode presents a document, not a source view

acceptanceCriteria.text: Opening a .md file mounts read mode by default
Mode toggle keyboard shortcut is wired

acceptanceCriteria: Opening a document with headings, emphasis, code, lists, tables, and links shows rendered output with no visible # / ** / backticks
Single click in read mode enters author mode with cursor placed at the click position
Cmd+E (or platform equivalent) toggles between modes in both directions
prefers-reduced-motion replaces the crossfade with an instant swap
Keyboard-only users can toggle modes without a mouse
items.groomingNotes: Load-bearing. Do not conflate with FEAT-015 (The Render animation) — that is a visual effect on the author surface; this is a distinct default view. The WYSIWYM decoration tree in src/wysiwym.ts is the right starting point for the mark-hiding logic. If scope creeps toward remembering per-file last mode or adding a settings override, split that off — this item ships the default and the transition only.

items.title: Wire document doctor diagnostics to editor gutter
items.intent: PRODUCT D-EDIT-1 makes the document doctor a core on-by-default feature of author mode. ARCHITECTURE §3.4 defines the doctor rules and says results are sent to the editor as diagnostics — underlines and gutter markers. Today markdown-core/src/doctor.rs implements the rules but nothing consumes them: no IPC command, no editor gutter, no underlines. Without this the editor is a dumb text box, not the smart author environment the product claims.
items.description:
Add a document_diagnose Tauri command that runs the doctor against the current document and returns a list of diagnostics (rule id, severity, byte range, message). Wire the command in the editor frontend to run after edits on a background debounce (500ms after last keystroke). Render diagnostics as CodeMirror gutter markers with hover tooltips showing the message, plus underline decorations on the offending range. The initial rules enabled are those already implemented in doctor.rs (heading hierarchy violations, broken relative links, duplicate headings, unclosed formatting).

Scope boundary: passive voice detection and the broader 'extended doctor rules' set live in FEAT-036 and do not ship here. This item wires existing rules end-to-end.
steps: Add #[tauri::command] fn document_diagnose(state, window) -> Vec<Diagnostic> in src-tauri/src/lib.rs
Define the Diagnostic struct (rule_id, severity, range, message) in markdown-core and re-export
Add runDoctor() TypeScript wrapper in src/ that invokes document_diagnose with debounce
Create a CodeMirror gutter extension that renders a marker per diagnostic line
Create a Decoration.mark extension that underlines the offending range
Add hover tooltip showing the diagnostic message
Wire the doctor extension into author mode (not read mode)
Verify diagnostics appear within 1s of a triggering edit and clear when the issue is fixed

acceptanceCriteria.text: Doctor gutter extension exists and is registered
Diagnostic type is defined in markdown-core

acceptanceCriteria: Opening a document with a skipped heading level (h1 → h3) shows a gutter marker on the offending line within 1s
Hovering the marker shows the rule message
Fixing the issue clears the marker within 1s
Diagnostics do not appear in read mode
The 500ms debounce does not block typing (keystroke latency stays within baseline)
items.groomingNotes: Doctor engine already exists in Rust — this is wiring, not algorithm work. Measure keystroke latency against baseline before and after; if the doctor run regresses typing, it needs to go on a worker (not the per-keystroke path). Extended rules (passive voice, etc.) remain in FEAT-036.

items.title: Fix auto-save debounce drift — 2s → 1s to match spec
items.intent: ARCHITECTURE §3.7 specifies a 1-second auto-save debounce. FEAT-025 shipped 2s. This is a spec/code drift with no rationale documented. Pick one — the 1s target was chosen for 'saving feels immediate' (D-PERF-1), so match the spec.
items.description: Change the auto-save debounce from 2000ms to 1000ms in the editor. Verify the content-hash skip still prevents write amplification. If 1s turns out to cause battery or I/O issues on any platform, update ARCHITECTURE.md §3.7 with the real number rather than leaving a silent drift.
steps: Find the auto-save debounce constant (likely in src/main.ts or src/editor.ts)
Change 2000 to 1000
Verify content-hash comparison still skips no-op saves on repeated trivial edits
Smoke-test on a large document that auto-save does not cause keystroke lag
acceptanceCriteria: Typing a character and pausing 1.2s writes the file; typing again within 1s of a write does not write twice
Large-document baseline keystroke latency stays within 110% of committed median
items.groomingNotes: Trivial. One-line change + verification. Keep FEAT-025's hash-skip intact.

items.title: Planned IPC commands — document_format, document_undo, document_redo, document_viewport, document_close
items.intent: ARCHITECTURE §3.8 lists these as planned and names them as the mechanism the frontend uses to reach formatter, undo, and viewport functionality in the Rust core. Today the frontend uses CodeMirror's built-in history for undo and has no way to trigger a full-document reformat from the core. Without these commands the core formatter and undo coalescing (§3.5) are unreachable from the UI.
items.description:
Add five Tauri commands to src-tauri/src/lib.rs, each backed by the corresponding core engine operation: document_format returns a list of mutations that fully reformats the document per the rule set in markdown-core/src/formatter.rs; document_undo and document_redo operate on the core's command history; document_viewport returns tokenized ranges for a byte-range viewport (sets up lazy tokenization for large files later); document_close releases per-window document state.

Scope boundary: document_diagnose is FEAT-050, not this item. Wiring document_format to a 'Format Document' menu item is in scope; wiring document_undo/redo to replace CodeMirror's history is a follow-up because it requires editor-side state reconciliation.
steps: Add #[tauri::command] fn document_format(state, window) -> Vec<Mutation> in src-tauri/src/lib.rs
Define Mutation struct in markdown-core (byte range + replacement text)
Add document_undo, document_redo, document_viewport, document_close commands
Wire document_format to a 'Format Document' entry in the platform menu and a Shift+Alt+F shortcut
Verify the formatter applies all five current rules (list continuation, table alignment, heading spacing, blank line sep, trailing whitespace trim)
Add a unit test per command in markdown-core
acceptanceCriteria: Running Format Document on a document with misaligned table pipes produces aligned output
document_viewport returns tokens for the requested byte range
items.groomingNotes: document_undo/redo replacing CodeMirror history is deliberately out of scope — only expose the commands; the editor keeps using history() for now. Revisit when undo coalescing (§3.5) becomes a user-visible need.

items.title: Hot-path formatting rules in editor with parity test against core
items.intent: ARCHITECTURE A-CORE-3 specifies that formatting rules which must respond within a single frame (Enter on a list, Tab in a list, table alignment on pipe typing) are duplicated in the editor frontend for zero-latency response, and that tests enforce identical output between the two implementations. Today the frontend relies on @codemirror/lang-markdown for basic list continuation; table alignment and heading spacing are not handled editor-side. This is the mechanism D-PERF-1 ('edits feel instant') relies on.
items.description:
Port the five formatter rules from markdown-core/src/formatter.rs into the editor in TypeScript, wired as CodeMirror transactions that fire on the triggering keystroke (Enter on list item, pipe in a table, etc.). Add a parity test suite that runs the same input corpus through both the TypeScript and Rust implementations and asserts identical output — this is the guardrail that keeps the duplication from drifting.

Scope boundary: this item is about the five shipped rules only. Any rule added to the core formatter after this ships must also land in the editor in the same item.
steps: Port each rule (list continuation, table alignment, heading spacing, blank line sep, trailing whitespace trim) into src/markdown-commands.ts or a new src/formatter.ts
Wire each rule to its triggering keystroke via CodeMirror transactionFilter or keymap
Create a parity test: shared corpus of (input, expected) pairs; run through both implementations in CI
Update ARCHITECTURE.md §3.3 if any rule intentionally diverges editor-side (none expected)

acceptanceCriteria.text: Parity test exists and runs in CI

acceptanceCriteria: Typing a pipe at end of table row auto-aligns all pipes in the table within one frame
Pressing Enter on a numbered list auto-continues the numbering
Pressing Enter on an empty list item exits the list
Parity test asserts zero diffs across the committed corpus
items.groomingNotes: Parity test is the load-bearing piece — without it the duplication drifts and bug reports become impossible to triage. Prefer running both implementations in a single Rust test via a JS runtime shim rather than running TS tests against a Rust subprocess.

items.title: UTF-8 BOM and non-UTF-8 encoding detection on open
items.intent: ARCHITECTURE §3.7 specifies UTF-8 as the default assumption, BOM detection for UTF-8-with-BOM files, and flagging of non-UTF-8 files to the user rather than silently mangling them. Line-ending preservation (D-FILE-3) is implemented; encoding preservation is not visibly implemented and tested. If a user opens a Windows-1252 or UTF-16 file we need to behave predictably.
items.description:
In markdown-core's file load path, detect the BOM (UTF-8, UTF-16-LE, UTF-16-BE). For UTF-8-with-BOM, strip the BOM on load and re-emit it on save. For UTF-16 or non-UTF-8 content, return an error to the Tauri shell which surfaces a non-modal banner offering to reopen with a specified encoding or cancel — not silent replacement, not silent mangling.

Scope boundary: this item ships BOM round-trip for UTF-8 and clear failure for non-UTF-8. Actual conversion/reopen with chosen encoding is a follow-up if users request it.
steps: Add encoding detection in markdown-core file open path: check first 3 bytes for UTF-8 BOM (EF BB BF), 2 bytes for UTF-16 BOMs
For UTF-8 BOM: strip on load, remember flag, re-emit BOM on save
For UTF-16 or invalid UTF-8: return EncodingError with detected encoding name
Surface EncodingError in the shell as a non-modal error banner (ties into D-ERR-1)
Write round-trip tests for UTF-8 and UTF-8-with-BOM
Write a test that non-UTF-8 input returns the expected error

acceptanceCriteria.text: BOM handling path exists in markdown-core
Encoding error surfaces to the shell

acceptanceCriteria: Opening a UTF-8-with-BOM file, editing, and saving produces a file whose first three bytes are still EF BB BF
Opening a UTF-8 file (no BOM), editing, and saving does not add a BOM
Opening a UTF-16-LE file surfaces a non-modal error banner; no silent mangling
items.groomingNotes: Do not pull in a charset detection crate for this — BOM-only detection is all §3.7 requires, and adding chardet-like inference invites silent misclassification. Explicit error for non-UTF-8 is the correct behavior.
